name: Leadenhall Market
description: This ornate Victorian marketplace was the setting for Diagon Alley and the Leaky Cauldron in the first ‘Harry Potter’ film.

name: Highgate Cemetery
description: London's creepiest cemetery was once the site of dueling magicians and mobs of stake-carrying vampire hunters.

name: The Ruins of St. Dunstan-in-the-East
description: One of the few remaining casualties of the London Blitz, this destroyed church has become an enchanting public garden.

name: God's Own Junkyard
description: A kaleidoscopic warehouse-maze of handmade neon signs.

name: Twinings Tea Shop
description: A 300-year-old tea shop that brought tea to the English people, not to mention the Queen herself.

name: The Viktor Wynd Museum of Curiosities, Fine Art & Natural History
description: This shop and gallery is a modern-day cabinet of curiosities, unassumingly positioned along an urban thoroughfare.

name: Cecil Court
description: A charming 17th-century alley is lined with secondhand bookstores and antiquarian shops.

name: Platform 9 3/4
description: Kings Cross Station pays tribute to its role in Britain's best-selling book series.

name: Goodwin's Court
description: An easily overlooked stretch of ornate window fronts and gaslight lamps that could be right from the pages of Dickens.

name: Magic Circle Museum
description: This collection of illusory paraphernalia has been culled from the history of London's premier magician's club.

name: Natural History Museum of London
description: Eighty million natural history specimens call this gargantuan museum home.

name: The Churchill War Rooms
description: The perfectly preserved underground rooms where Churchill plotted the war against Germany.

name: 221b Baker Street
description: The popularity of Sherlock Holmes led to the creation of his fictional address, turning the orderly London street numbers askew.

name: Kensal Green Cemetery and Catacombs
description: Winding paths and ramshackle mausoleums in the oldest "park cemetery" in London.

name: Novelty Automation
description: A tongue-in-cheek arcade where the games are based on everyday life.

name: Banksy's 'Designated Graffiti Area'
description: Banksy gives themself artistic license with this piece of London street art.

name: Word on the Water
description: This 1920s Dutch barge is now a floating bookstore.

name: The Sherlock Holmes Museum
description: This London pub hides a peculiar secret: a recreation of the rooms shared at 221b Baker Street by Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson.

name: The Mail Rail
description: Hidden beneath the streets of London is a secret subway that only carried mail.

name: The King's Cross Ice Well
description: A subterranean ice warehouse that has lain hidden beneath central London for over a century is now open to the public.

name: Kyoto Garden
description: A little slice of Japan and its aesthetics in London's Holland Park.

name: Seven Noses of Soho
description: Several plaster noses are hidden in plain sight around London's Soho neighborhood.

name: The Old Curiosity Shop
description: The quaint little store that is said to have inspired a famous Dickens novel was only given its name after the book was released.

name: The London Dungeon
description: One part local history, 99 parts gruesome haunted house.

name: Crossbones Graveyard
description: This South London cemetery holds the remains of more than 15,000 people.

name: Grant Museum of Zoology
description: The only university zoological museum in London houses extinct animals, bizarre natural history specimens, and a Micrarium of microscopic creatures.

name: Stables Market
description: Catacombs turned marketplace.

name: Greenwich Foot Tunnel
description: A 1,215-foot tunnel transports pedestrians beneath the River Thames.

name: Temple of Mithras
description: Rebuilt remains of a temple to Roman god Mithras.

name: Traffic Light Tree
description: A student driver's nightmare.

name: Café in the Crypt
description: This coffee shop lies hidden beneath a historic church.

name: Hoxton Street Monster Supplies
description: A special shop catering to the needs of things that go bump in the night, and also helps kids become writers.

name: Nunhead Cemetery
description: A vast overgrown cemetery, once known as one of London's "Magnificent Seven."

name: Temple Church
description: An unusual round church in London with a Templar past.

name: Hunterian Museum
description: The anatomical-pathological collection of a man who changed surgery.

name: Leake Street Graffiti Tunnel
description: A hands-off space for graffiti artists to hone and display their skills.

name: The Morpeth Arms
description: A historic London pub with prison cells in the cellar and a "spy room" on the second floor.

name: Wellcome Collection & Library
description: The curios of pharmacist and collector Henry Wellcome.

name: The Clink Prison Museum
description: An incarceration museum on the spot of possibly the oldest prison in England.

name: Ruined Victorian Folly
description: Remnants of a Victorian garden feature sitting in public woodland.

name: London's Original and All-Inspiring Coffee House
description: The site of London's first coffee house has been serving refreshments of one kind or another for 360 years.

name: Old Operating Theatre Museum & Herb Garret
description: In this cobwebbed old operating theater, you can practically hear the screams of the unanesthetized patients.

name: Hyde Park Pet Cemetery
description: The final resting place to 300 Victorian-era pets hides inside the city park.

name: Lullaby Factory
description: Wedged between two buildings at the Great Ormond Street Hospital is this secret music installation.

name: The House of Dreams Museum
description: This wildly-tiled artist's palace is a living and growing account of the creator's life, loves, and labors.

name: Barbican Conservatory
description: This hidden green space is the city's second largest conservatory.

name: Whispering Gallery at St Paul’s Cathedral
description: Hear the quietest sound from across the dome.

name: Sir John Soane's Museum
description: A cluttered and astounding collection of antiquities and curiosities.

name: Euston's Lost Tunnels
description: A network of concealed passageways lies within a busy London Underground station.

name: Guildhall's Underground Roman Amphitheater
description: A 2000-year-old Roman amphitheater lies just below Guildhall Yard.

name: Paddington Bear Statue
description: After nearly 60 years, there's still a bear at Paddington Station looking for help.

name: The Embassy of the Republic of Texas
description: An alleyway plaque is all that remains of the independent Texas' envoy that forgot to pay their rent for over a century.

name: London's Lilliputian Police Station
description: London's smallest police station is barely the size of a phone booth.

name: The Mayflower Pub
description: The London pub overlooks where the Mayflower was moored before setting sail for the New World.

name: The Cartoon Museum
description: A collection of comics, graphic novels, animation and caricatures devoted to exploring this often overlooked art form.

name: Ruins of the Crystal Palace
description: The remains of Crystal Palace Great Exhibition of 1851.

name: The Wildgoose Memorial Library
description: A mysterious artist-run cabinet of curiosities and library.

name: Earl's Court Police Box
description: The Metropolitan Police refurbished the blue box (perhaps not coincidentally) the same year "Doctor Who" returned to TV screens.

name: Gordon Museum of Pathology
description: One of the world's largest collections of pathological specimens.

name: Horniman Museum and Gardens
description: A Victorian natural history and ethnographic museum with wonderful turn-of-the-century, science-book-esque evolution displays.

name: Chelsea Physic Garden
description: London's old-school healing garden.

name: Philpot Lane Mice Sculpture
description: A mysterious pair of mice eating a piece of cheese is London's smallest public sculpture.

name: The Real Greenwich Prime Meridian
description: Thanks to modern navigational tools we now know that the true prime meridian runs through a park next door.

name: Algerian Coffee Stores
description: Since 1887, this old-school shop has stacked its shelves with extraordinary coffees and teas.

name: Darwin Centre Spirit Collection
description: Repository for more than 22 million pickled specimens.

name: The Monty Python Foot
description: The television series' iconic giant foot was borrowed from this classical painting.

name: Tower Hamlets Cemetery Park
description: A historic London cemetery now serves as a nature park incorporating gravestones & memorial statues.

name: London Wall
description: Scattered throughout London are ancient remnants of the city's former bounding wall.

name: The Tower Ravens
description: Six ravens are kept captive (but well-fed) at the Tower of London to prevent the fall of the Crown.

name: Billingsgate Roman House and Baths
description: A Roman spa buried deep beneath the City of London.

name: The Imperial War Museum London
description: This massive collection of British military artifacts includes the heartbreaking personal letters of the men in the trenches of WWI.

name: Finch and Co Antiques
description: English antique dealers with a taste for the unusual.

name: David Bowie Graffiti Mural
description: An artistic tribute to the famous singer, just around the corner from where he was born.

name: Village Underground
description: Artists and musicians now rule over this former rail station.

name: Saint Bartholomew's Hospital Pathology Museum
description: A collection dedicated to the treatment of disease, held in London's oldest hospital.

name: Broad Street Cholera Pump
description: The John Snow Memorial marks the epicenter of London's 1854 cholera epidemic.

name: Crystal Palace Dinosaurs
description: Abstract dinosaur models in London.

name: Peter Pan Statue
description: A statue marks the exact spot where The Boy Who Never Grows Up made his first literary appearance.

name: Masonic Lodge of the Andaz Hotel
description: Hidden behind a wall for decades, this masonic lodge was only rediscovered during renovations.

name: London Necropolis Railway Station
description: A closed London rail station created to cart away the dead still bears the marks of attempts to make it less morbid.

name: Museum of Brands
description: Collection dedicated to brands, packaging and advertising.

name: Brompton Cemetery
description: The sixth of London's "Magnificent Seven" cemeteries, and a source of unlikely inspiration to Beatrix Potter.

name: Victoria & Albert Museum Dining Rooms
description: Revel in the Victorian splendor of the world's oldest eating establishment inside a museum.

name: Speakers' Corner
description: London's last remaining public soapbox site has seen speeches from Karl Marx, Vladmir Lenin, and George Orwell.

name: ‘Out of Order’
description: A domino effect created with Britain’s iconic telephone boxes.

name: The Ten Bells Pub
description: Mary Kelly was last seen here before she was murdered by Jack the Ripper.

name: The Churchill Arms
description: This pub was 238 years old when it decided to revamp its image: It started serving Thai food.

name: Petrie Museum of Egyptian Archaeology
description: An small and easily overlooked archaeology museum chock full of Egyptian artifacts.

name: Public Standards of Length
description: 19th-century scientists would make the pilgrimage here to verify the precision of their measuring sticks.

name: Hampstead Pergola
description: This secluded walkway overlooks a magnificently wild London green space.

name: St Olave Hart Street
description: A hidden treasure in London with a rich medieval history, and the resting place of many luminaries.

name: Pelicans of St James's Park
description: Giant, friendly pelicans in Central London, the most unlikely of places.

name: The Attendant, Fitzrovia
description: Drink your espresso at a Victorian-era urinal in this underground-restroom-turned-coffee-bar.

name: Crossness Pumping Station
description: This beautiful Victorian ironwork masterpiece was built to keep London from smelling like poop.

name: Freud Museum London
description: The pioneer of psychoanalysis spent the final year of his life here, still seeing patients on that famous couch.

name: Ziggy Stardust Plaque
description: This London plaque marks the spot where David Bowie's famous alien made his debut.

name: Clock of the Long Now: Prototype 1
description: A clock designed to run with perfect accuracy for 10,000 years.

name: Crystal Palace Subway
description: A relic of Victorian grandeur hides beneath a busy South London road.

name: Lindow Man
description: A naturally mummified 2,000-year-old man.

name: The Star Tavern
description: The bar where "The Great Train Robbery" was planned was once a nexus of high society and low morals.

name: London Underground Labyrinths
description: A labyrinth hides in plain sight at each of the city's 270 Tube stations.

name: 'Treatment Rooms' Mosaic House
description: This home on a quiet suburban street is completely covered with vibrant mosaics.

name: Camera Museum
description: This museum tucked below a charming cafe gives a brief snapshot of photographic history.

name: Site of Execution Dock
description: Where condemned pirates met their grisly end at the gallows on the River Thames.

name: Rolling Bridge
description: This unique bridge curls up like a caterpillar to let boats pass and then unfurls again to span a small canal.

name: The London Stone
description: This legendary stone is of unknown origin, and throughout history has been a literal touchstone for British leaders.

name: Lock & Co.
description: Claiming to be the oldest hat store in the world, this historic haberdashery had a hand (and a head) in the creation of the Bowler.

name: Parkland Walk
description: Following the trail of an old metro line, this scenic path is haunted by a creepy spriggan statue.

name: The Lost River Fleet
description: The largest of London's subterranean rivers and once a mad, bad center of London life.

name: Temple Bar Memorial Dragon
description: The fierce beast perches atop a pedestal marking where the historic City of London gates once stood.

name: Battersea Power Station
description: Pop culture has helped to keep this iconic British building from being destroyed.

name: Rules
description: Classic game cookery takes center stage at what's widely considered London's oldest restaurant.

name: Archie the Giant Squid
description: The monster at the center of the  Darwin Center Spirit Collection.

name: Monument to Hodge the Cat
description: Dictionary Johnson’s favorite feline.

name: St. Nicholas Church Memento Mori
description: These morbid carvings remind churchgoers of the inevitability of death.

name: Hidden Cells of Newgate Prison
description: Remnants of cells from the most notorious London prison may lie in the basement of a rare Victorian Gin Palace.

name: Columbia Road Shops and Flower Market
description: The Columbia Road Flower Market is now open every Sunday, rain or shine.

name: Abney Park Cemetery
description: A historic arboretum and non-conformist, non-denominational park cemetery.

name: Queen's Diamond Jubilee Galleries
description: This long-hidden space above Westminster Abbey now displays the battle gear of the legendary King Henry V.

name: St Bride's Church & Charnel House
description: This landmark church concealed a crypt packed with bones that was discovered thanks to the London Blitz.

name: Michael Faraday's Laboratory
description: The lab where the scientist made his groundbreaking discoveries is preserved on the basement floor of the Faraday Museum.

name: The Burlington Arcade Beadles
description: This luxury shopping lane has been protected by its own tiny police force for over a century.

name: The Museum of Everything (Moving Location)
description: London's largest outsider art museum.

name: British Library Treasures Gallery
description: The library houses an incredible collection of some of the world's most impressive books and manuscripts.

name: Tyburn Tree Marker
description: Never actually a tree at all, this spot was the site of London's public hangings for nearly 600 years.

name: Audley Square Spy Lamp Post
description: This overlooked street light once served as a KGB dead letter box.

name: The Dove
description: A glorious pub known for a spiteful printing feud and its famous typeface lost—then found—at the bottom of the Thames.

name: Cabaret Mechanical Theatre
description: Traveling collection of amazing automata.

name: Museum of the Home
description: Explore nearly 400 years of English middle-class home life.

name: 'The Naked Ladies'
description: No one knows who created the statues or if they are arranged properly—let alone the artwork's real name.

name: David Bowie's Childhood Home
description: The childhood home of Earth's alien chameleon is a humble Brixton flat.

name: Trafalgar Square Imperial Measurements
description: These official units of measurement—including chains and perches—lie hidden beneath tourists' feet in a popular London hub.

name: The London Library
description: More than a million titles fill the labyrinthine shelves of this prestigious private library.

name: Tulip Stairs
description: The first geometric self-supporting spiral stairs in Britain are a truly beautiful sight.

name: Leighton House Museum
description: This artist's home turned gallery/museum preserves the creator's love of "Eastern" influences.

name: Allies Sculpture
description: Bronze sculpture celebrating the relationship between Churchill and Roosevelt.

name: Two Princes Staircase
description: Richard III supposedly disposed of his nephews' bodies here in an effort to seal his claim to the throne.

name: Strawberry Hill
description: In Twickenham sits a small whitewashed castle that inspired Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein.'

name: Hoa Hakananai'a
description: The "lost friend” is the most famous of the six moai statues that were removed from Easter Island.

name: Longplayer
description: A musical composition 1000 years in length plays at the Trinity Buoy Wharf in London.

name: The Deadhouse
description: Tombstones and enigmatic inscriptions hide within a vault beneath a popular London arts venue.

name: The Theatre
description: Two plaques commemorate the site of Shakespeare's first theatre.

name: The Champion
description: This 19th-century pub features stained-glass windows celebrating Victorian-era “champions.”

name: Sir Richard Burton's Tent-Tomb
description: Eclectic final resting place of the controversial explorer.

name: William Wallace Memorial
description: A plaque hangs near the execution place of the Scottish Independence leader famously depicted in "Braveheart."

name: Yeoman Warders Club
description: There's an exclusive Beefeaters pub hidden in the Tower of London.

name: Horniman Aquarium
description: A wonderfully eclectic Victorian aquarium lies in the basement of the Horniman Museum.

name: Electric Avenue
description: "We gonna rock down to Electric Avenue, and then we'll take it higher..."

name: Bunhill Fields
description: A famous burial ground with a history both grim and literary.

name: V&A Museum of Childhood
description: A museum wholly devoted to juvenescence.

name: West Norwood Cemetery
description: A Victorian park cemetery, standing on the site of what was once the Great North Wood.

name: The First Public Drinking Fountain
description: Public access to clean drinking water was an instant hit among the masses.

name: Traitors' Gate
description: The watery entrance for condemned prisoners heading to the Tower of London is still visible along the Thames.

name: Queen Mary's Garden
description: The rose garden in Regent's Park where Pongo and Perdita met for the first time in Disney's "101 Dalmatians."

name: Viking Tombstone
description: This Viking gravestone carved in a rare runic style was dug up from a London churchyard.

name: The Executioner's Ax
description: Tucked away in the Tower of London is the weapon once used to execute high-profile prisoners.

name: Houseboats of Regents Canal
description: A community of artists and entrepreneurs seeks a simpler life on London's waterways.

name: Signs of Old Lombard Street
description: The last four extravagant street signs of London's historic banking street.

name: Tower of London's Ceremony of the Keys
description: The ritual has been performed uninterrupted for nearly 700 years.

name: Supper Club Tube
description: A decommissioned tube carriage within a London museum hosts a regular pop-up supper club.

name: Alfred Hitchcock's London Flat
description: One of the greatest movie directors of all time lived and worked in this London flat from 1926 to 1939.

name: Difference Engine #2
description: Charles Babbage's proto-computer, painstakingly brought to life.

name: The Ghost of a Flea
description: William Blake claimed to have painted this gothic masterpiece after encountering this nightmarish being in a vision.

name: 'Monument for a Dead Parrot'
description: The sculpture of the deceased cockatoo flops atop its personal plinth.

name: Boadicea and Her Daughters
description: A statue of the legendary Celtic warrior queen who fought the Roman invaders stands in one of the cities she once destroyed.

name: The Cathedral of Sewage
description: This elaborate Byzantine-style building was designed for a not-so-pretty purpose.

name: London Transport Museum Depot
description: The majority of the museum's large collection is held in a storage space open to the public a few times a year.

name: Down Street Underground Station
description: The secret wartime bunker built on the platforms of an abandoned Tube station.

name: Roman Wharf Timber
description: The 2,000-year-old beam is tucked within the pedestrian entrance to the old London Bridge.

name: Love Locks of East London
description: Lovers proclaim their unbreakable bond by leaving their names on locks fastened onto the Shoreditch fence in East London.

name: Brunel Museum Grand Entrance Hall
description: The first tunnel to be dug beneath a river, repurposed as an art and museum space.

name: London Transport Museum
description: Its collection includes a broad array of social, geographic, graphic design, and political history.

name: William Blake's Home
description: The townhouse where the English poet created some of his most influential work has been preserved in tribute.

name: The Navigators
description: Ship-sized kinetic sculptural fantasy.

name: Elfin Oak
description: This ancient oak plays host to carvings of magical elves, gnomes, witches, and other creatures prominent in local folklore.

name: The Cockpit
description: A friendly pub with a sordid past in cock fighting.

name: Tomb of the Unknown London Girl
description: The remains of an ancient Roman teenager are memorialized at the base of a modern London landmark.

name: Urine Deflectors of Fleet Street
description: One of the few remaining examples of 19th-century London’s attempt to clean up its reputation.

name: K2 Telephone Boxes
description: The wooden telephone box hidden away in London's Piccadilly was the original prototype of the iconic kiosk.

name: Black Cats of Carreras Cigarette Factory
description: Black cat statues guard this temple-like Egyptian revival factory.

name: Leinster Gardens False Facades
description: You'd never know the houses at 23-24 Leinster Gardens were fakes—until you see the train tracks on the other side.

name: Replica of the Golden Hinde
description: This one has sailed five times as far as Sir Francis Drake's famous ship.

name: Brown Dog Statue
description: A memorial to a dog that inspired rioting in the streets of London.

name: Cabmen’s Shelter in Russell Square
description: Originally built for London’s cab drivers, this bright green hut now serves anyone a hot drink or meal.

name: Dinner by Heston Blumenthal
description: Historic cuisine meets fine dining in a hotel-restaurant overlooking Hyde Park.

name: Admiral's House
description: An eccentric 18th century naval officer built a ship's quarterdeck on his roof, the inspiration for Mary Poppins's "Admiral Boom."

name: Metropolitan Police Coat Hook
description: Installed at a chaotic intersection, this gave London traffic cops a place to hang their heavy woolen coats.

name: Eltham Palace
description: Art Deco and “Wrenaissance” mash-up, this recently restored royal residence has been home to kings and comes with secret monkey passages included.

name: The London Beer Flood
description: On October 17, 1814, a 15-ft. wave of beer ran rampant through the streets of London.

name: Hackney City Farm
description: A flourishing garden with pigs, donkeys, goats, rabbits, guinea pigs, chickens, ducks, and sheep.

name: The Charterhouse Black Death Skeleton
description: This historic building displays the remains of a victim of the devastating 14th-century plague.

name: Tower Bridge Chimney
description: An architectural oddity hides in plain sight on this iconic London bridge.

name: Jeremy Bentham's Auto-Icon
description: The remains of the great moral philosopher reside in a university in London.

name: Handel & Hendrix in London
description: The next-door homes of two very different musicians, George Frideric Handel and Jimi Hendrix.

name: Rubens Ceiling at the Banqueting House
description: The sumptuous ceiling was one of the last things Charles I saw before his execution.

name: London's Stretcher Railings
description: The fences outside some of South London's estates are made from recycled World War II stretchers.

name: Pink Floyd Plaque
description: This London marker celebrates the spot where the world-famous English band was founded.

name: The Troubadour
description: A 1950s beatnik coffee house, turned 60s counterculture hotspot, turned modern music venue.

name: Clown Egg Register
description: An archive of ceramic eggs painted to record clowns' personal makeup designs.

name: 575 Wandsworth Road
description: What started as a way to address damp basement walls turned into a house filled with intricate hand-carved designs.

name: Vauxhall Bridge's Miniature St. Paul's Cathedral
description: A tiny replica of the iconic London landmark hides on the side of the steel structure.

name: William Blake Mosaics
description: This open-air mosaic gallery honoring an English artist took hundreds of volunteers more than seven years to create.

name: Athena the Owl
description: The Florence Nightingale Museum is home to the famous nurse's most treasured possession: her beloved pet owl.

name: Sir Ernest Shackleton's Crow's Nest
description: The barrel-made lookout from Shackleton's final ship is tucked away in the crypt of one of London's oldest churches.

name: The Soho Square Hut
description: The Tudor-style cottage in the middle of the square is not quite what it seems.

name: Water Dragons of Kew Gardens
description: These docile dragons are ideal inhabitants for the conservatory greenhouses.

name: Foundling Museum
description: Museum dedicated to the UK's first charity for abandoned children, with keepsakes of the mothers who left them behind.

name: Monument to the Great Fire of London
description: The commemorative stone column conceals a secret laboratory.

name: Smith Square World War II Ghost Sign
description: A faded reminder of what was once a common sight in London during the Blitz.

name: Container City
description: Putting the "mod" in "modular" since 2000.

name: York Water Gate
description: These ornate arches show how the course of the River Thames has changed.

name: Books for Cooks
description: This bookstore serves recipes from the cookbooks on sale.

name: Richmond Park
description: This beautiful deer park was built so King Charles I and his court could go hunting while escaping a deadly plague outbreak.

name: Dick Whittington’s Cat
description: A monument to the thrice mayor of London who according to lore made his fortune by selling his rat-chasing cat.

name: Chained Library at Chelsea Old Church
description: This small collection of rare books was uniquely locked up for safe keeping.

name: Tongue & Brisket
description: This third-generation institution is one of the last in London that still cures its own salt beef for epic sandwiches.

name: The Clockmakers' Museum
description: This museum inside a museum tells the story of London's timepieces.

name: Carting Lane Sewer Lamp
description: The pretty iron lamp is a reminder of the architecture underneath London's streets.

name: The Cornhill Devils
description: These Victorian era devil sculptures were designed to spite a local vicar.

name: I Goat
description: Spitalfields' regal market goat is said to be a monument to the area's migrant population but most are pretty sure it's just a goat.

name: Mary Anning's Plesiosaur
description: This marine reptile was discovered by one of the 19th century's greatest fossil hunters.

name: Coade Stone Caryatids
description: A measuring mishap led to these artificial stone ladies losing their stomachs.

name: Hungerford Footbridge Skateboard Graveyard
description: The broken skateboards that became a poignant memorial to a murdered skater.

name: Aldgate Pump
description: This pump on the bad side of Victorian London betrayed a horrifying revelation about the water supply.

name: Get Stuffed
description: Taxidermied animals galore, by appointment only.

name: Tomb Of Hannah Courtoy
description: According to legend, this tomb holds the secrets to time travel.

name: One Tree Hill
description: A historic hill of tall tales, with one rather special tree.

name: 'A Conversation With Oscar Wilde'
description: An unusual ode to the late Irish playwright, complete with a squiggly bust and coffin-shaped bench.

name: Animals in War Memorial
description: A unique tribute to the millions of animals who served and died for the British military.

name: Kirkaldy Testing Museum
description: A collection of machines designed to break things for science.

name: Ragged School Museum
description: A Victorian school for poverty stricken waifs has been preserved as a museum to their donated education.

name: The Four Mother Goddesses
description: One of the figures in this Celtic sculpture continues to puzzle archaeologists.

name: The Tower of London Menagerie
description: These wire animal sculptures commemorate the exotic inhabitants that once called the Tower of London home.

name: Whitefriars Crypt
description: Buried for centuries, this hidden cellar is all that remains of London's medieval Whitefriars Monastery.

name: Osterley Bookshop
description: Above an abandoned Underground station, this charming bookstore is home to strange antiquities and literary works.

name: Polly at Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese
description: The taxidermied remains of a once-great parrot.

name: Tower Bridge’s Dead Man’s Hole
description: This unassuming alcove in London’s famous Tower Bridge once served to capture, store, and display dead bodies from the River Thames.

name: Dirty Dick's
description: This pub boasts a curious history and may have inspired a Dickens character.

name: WWT London Wetland Centre
description: London's marvelous wildfowl reserve along the Thames.

name: The Blind Beggar
description: This East End pub gained notoriety as the site where gangster Ronnie Kray publicly shot a rival.

name: The Lewis Chessmen
description: These mysterious Viking chess pieces spent centuries hidden on a remote Scottish island.

name: Cleopatra's Needle Shrapnel Scars
description: This ancient Egyptian obelisk still bears the wounds of World War I.

name: The College of Psychic Studies
description: This educational institution and reference library specialize in the psychic arts and mediumship.

name: London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Vectors of Disease Frieze
description: The Art Deco artwork depicts gilded carriers of deadly illnesses.

name: 36 Blythe Road
description: Where Aleister Crowley attempted to seize the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn in a mask and a kilt.

name: Britain’s Oldest Door
description: At almost 1,000 years old, this is the oldest known door in all of Britain.

name: The Ferryman's Seat
description: The last remaining example of the boatmen's perches that once dotted the south bank of the Thames.

name: 'Titania and Bottom'
description: This macabre painting of a Shakespearean scene looks like something out of a nightmare.

name: Postman's Park: Memorial to Heroic Self Sacrifice
description: A quiet memorial to those who died saving others in the heart of the City of London.

name: Millennium Bridge Tiny Chewing Gum Art
description: The bridge is a secret gallery of discarded gum that has been transformed into miniature masterpieces.

name: Westland London
description: High-end antique collection housed in a centuries-old church.

name: Queen Caroline's Bath
description: The plunge pool is a surviving relic of a rowdy royal residence.

name: Marc Bolan's Rock Shrine
description: A roadside shrine to a late rock god only remains thanks to the unyielding efforts of his fans.

name: Brown Hart Gardens
description: It is still illegal to "quarrel" at this unlikely urban oasis built on top of an electrical substation.

name: Church of Notre Dame de France
description: Colorful murals by Jean Cocteau hide within this peaceful London sanctuary.

name: S.O.E. Monument
description: A bronze bust commemorates the heroism of the secret agents who led covert operations against the Nazis.

name: British Dental Museum
description: The collection contains over 25,000 items and details the painful dental practices of the past.

name: The Fortnum & Mason Clock
description: At the top of the hour, figures of the London department store's two co-founders emerge and bow to each other.

name: Peace Garden at Tavistock Square
description: A quiet, semi-secret square with statues of Mahatma Gandhi, Virginia Woolf, and a conscientious objectors stone.

name: Michael Faraday Memorial
description: Shiny Brutalist box commemorates a pioneer of electricity and houses a railway transformer.

name: Remains of the Old London Bridge
description: Only a few pieces remain of the structure that carried people and goods across the Thames for some 600 years.

name: Quantum Cloud
description: Sculptor Antony Gormley used chaos theory and fractal growth to design his tallest creation.

name: The Aurochs Skull
description: The gigantic skull of a long-extinct species that once roamed the forests of England and Ireland.

name: Chequer Street Wooden Paving
description: This may be the last of London's wooden pavements, which were once a common sight in the city.

name: Anaesthesia Heritage Centre
description: This knockout museum lies in a sleepy corner of London.

name: Horizon Insects
description: London's first edible insect farm offers facility tours and cooking classes.

name: Street Cat Bob Memorial Statue
description: This life-size monument honors the legacy of a famous literary feline.

name: British Optical Association Museum
description: The world's oldest optical museum is an odd collection where the exhibits look at you.

name: The Burney Relief
description: This bewitching Babylonian goddess haunts a hallway of the British Museum.

name: 10 Adam Street
description: England's most famous front door has an accidental doppelganger.

name: Christchurch Greyfriars Church Garden
description: The ruins of a church destroyed by both the Great Fire of London and the Blitz, now turned into a peaceful garden.

name: Walthamstow Market
description: Though it's not the largest, this one-kilometer outdoor market is reportedly the longest in Europe.

name: The Coronation Stone
description: Legend has it that seven Anglo-Saxon kings were coronated on this stone, located in the appropriately-named town of Kingston.

name: Kennel Club Library
description: The canine-themed book collection claims to be Europe's largest dog library.

name: Panzer’s Deli & Grocery
description: Order bagels and lox from an iconic Jewish delicatessen with nearly a century of history behind it.

name: The Great Bed of Ware
description: This intricately carved and hilariously huge bed was such a famous symbol both Shakespeare and Byron used it in their writing.

name: Marianne North Gallery at Kew Gardens
description: A gallery specifically dedicated to the botanical illustrations of a remarkable traveling lady.

name: The Golden Boy at Pye Corner
description: A portly statue of a golden boy commemorates an unusual cause of the Great Fire of London: the sin of gluttony.

name: Memorial to the Women of World War II
description: The bronze uniforms commemorate the vital role of women during World War II.

name: Beigel Bake
description: Order a salt beef-loaded beigel at any hour of the day or night at this East End institution.

name: Shoreditch Town Hall
description: A recently restored Shoreditch landmark with ties to Jack the Ripper.

name: London's Rose-Ringed Parakeets
description: Legend says these colorful, invasive residents are the descendants of birds released by Jimi Hendrix.

name: Museum of London Docklands
description: A museum that chronicles the interesting and often dark history of the London docklands, once one of the major trading ports of the world.

name: Henry VIII Gatehouse Statue
description: London's only public statue of the much-married monarch adorns the gate to St Bartholomew’s Hospital.

name: London Silver Vaults
description: Behind one of the world's most impenetrable vaults is a massive collection of retail silver.

name: 'The Ambassadors'
description: A mysterious shape catches the eye in this 16th-century painting.

name: Walbrook Skulls
description: It's a mystery why these remains were found with no traces of their bodies in sight.

name: Dirac Equation
description: The "beautiful" equation predicting the movement of all electromagnetic particles is engraved in front of Newton's tomb.

name: Isokon Flats
description: An exercise in radical design and social living that became the epicenter of Soviet spy networks in London.

name: Whitechapel Fatberg Manhole Cover
description: It celebrates a very stinky task and salutes London's fatberg-vanquishing team.

name: 'An Allegory of Man'
description: This mysterious and unique painting is filled with symbolism.

name: Brixton Windmill
description: A fully functional, 200-year-old remnant of the time when Brixton was mostly fields.

name: Aldwych Tube Station
description: This Underground station has been a popular filming location for decades, even before it closed.

name: Benjamin Franklin House
description: One of America's founding fathers lived in this London lodging between 1757 and 1775.

name: Strand Lane 'Roman Baths'
description: Victorian-era Londoners really wanted to believe this 17th-century cistern was actually an ancient artifact.

name: Honey & Co. Bloomsbury
description: Feast on Middle Eastern mezze from an Israeli expat duo.

name: Scenes from the Frost Fair
description: Murals of the wild parties once held on the frozen River Thames.

name: ‘Monument to the Unknown Artist’
description: The London statue that imitates passersby.

name: Victoria and Albert Museum WWII Battle Scars
description: The pockmarks across the building's facade are remnants of the Blitz.

name: Queer Britain
description: A small museum that celebrates the history and culture of the LGBTQ community in England.

name: 'Tapestry of Life' Mural
description: This peeling mural of Adam and Eve was inspired by the greenhouses at Kew Gardens.

name: Angela Flanders Perfumer
description: A boutique perfumery fashioned to look like a Victorian-era shop.

name: John Harrison's Marine Chronometers
description: The handiwork of a underdog clockmaker who solved an impossible problem for an Empire, and forever revolutionized seafaring.

name: Cittie of Yorke
description: This pub has roots dating back to 1430 and serves as the setting for multiple Charles Dickens stories.

name: Lime Wharf
description: Residential building on the Regents Canal is a breeding ground for creativity and innovation.

name: Ye Olde Mitre
description: Down a pint of ale where the Bishop of Ely's servants slept in 1546.

name: Memorial to Mahomet Weyonomon
description: A sculpture outside London's oldest cathedral honors the Mohegan chief who died while petitioning the king.

name: St Augustine's Tower
description: This medieval church tower has maintained its clockworks since the 16th century.

name: Charlton House
description: The grounds of this Jacobean estate contain one of the oldest mulberry trees in England.

name: The Wallace Collection Armory
description: An eccentric Victorian aristocrat's enormous collection of medieval armor and weapons from around the world.

name: The Oval Space
description: This former medical factory gives home to cutting edge arts in the shadow of a gas works.

name: Great Ormond Street Hospital
description: Children's hospital in London that operates on royalties received from Peter Pan, which J.M. Barrie signed over in 1929.

name: Mount Street Gardens
description: Tucked away behind the mansions of Mayfair, this picturesque garden is a hidden London treasure with a spooky past.

name: Joseph Grimaldi Park
description: An unassuming London park is actually a pilgrimage site for modern clowns.

name: The Fan Museum
description: This museum is dedicated solely to this millennia-old handheld cooling device.

name: Epstein's Medical Sculptures
description: A series of carved figures that scandalized British society.

name: Former Decca Studios
description: This studio was home to David Bowie's first single, even if it did turn away the Beatles.

name: V&A Dog Memorials
description: Hidden in the courtyard of the Victoria and Albert Museum is a little-known tribute to two celebrity pooches.

name: King’s Observatory
description: Built by George III to observe the transit of Venus, this Georgian gem is a unique survivor in the Arcadian landscape of Richmond upon Thames.

name: Wolf-God of Woodeaton
description: A gruesome and mysterious ancient sculpture of a pagan deity from the Roman occupation of Celtic Britain.

name: The Vincent Street Fireplace
description: A lasting remnant of the London Blitz.

name: Hand of St. Etheldreda
description: One of the oldest remaining Catholic churches in England is home to the hand of a 7th-century saint.

name: River Tyburn Conduit
description: A plaque marks the underground site of one of London's "lost rivers."

name: Beauchamp Tower
description: Graffiti from the 16th and 17th centuries cover the walls of this part of the Tower of London.

name: Charing Cross Mural
description: The medieval woodcut-style artwork chronicles life in medieval London.

name: St. Sepulchre's Execution Bell
description: On the eve of a condemned prisoner's death, this bell rang 12 times outside their cell.

name: Ernest Shackleton Statue
description: The famous polar explorer stands outside the Royal Geographical Society's headquarters.

name: St. Leonard Church of Shoreditch
description: Historic church where a number of Shakespeare's actors are buried.

name: 'Potteries and Piggeries' Bottle Kiln
description: A relic from one of London's worst 19th-century slums.

name: Premises Studio Music Studios and Cafe
description: London's most popular recording studio, located in the capital's East End, runs partly on solar power and has played host to some of the world's biggest acts.

name: Tower of London Barbary Lion Skulls
description: The bones of two medieval royal lions were discovered at the historic fortress.

name: Fitzroy House
description: This historic London residence was once the womb of Scientology.

name: Pudding Lane
description: The Great Fire of London and the regulation of one-way streets both started in this alley.

name: Hyman Archive
description: The world’s largest magazine collection was born from a single shelf in a teen’s bedroom.

name: Sir John Franklin Expedition Memorial
description: A tribute to the intrepid explorer and his crew who perished in terrible conditions on an ill-fated expedition to the Arctic.

name: Severndroog Castle
description: A monolithic monument in Greater London.

name: Barnet Physic Well
description: A mineral spring that became all the rage in 17th-century England.

name: Mold Gold Cape
description: This intriguing Bronze Age artifact spent centuries hidden within a Welsh faerie hill.

name: Sherling Backstage Walkway
description: A hidden path at the National Theatre lets you look behind the scenes of the latest productions.

name: Parliamentary Division Bells
description: A network of bells in the pubs and restaurants around Westminster alerts politicians when it's time to cast their votes.

name: The Royal Philatelic Collection
description: The British monarchy's stamp collection is one of the most impressive in all of Europe revealing generations of royal nerdery.

name: Cheapside Plane Tree
description: What might be the oldest living tree in London has survived fires, bombings, and estate agents.

name: King Henry's Mound
description: It is against the law to obstruct the view from the top of this English burial mound.

name: A Geological Map of England and Wales and Part of Scotland
description: William Smith's iconic "map that changed the world" is hidden behind a curtain in the Geological Society building.

name: Statue of Guy the Gorilla
description: A sculpture of a famous ape who was once a beloved household name in London.

name: The Ancient House
description: One of London's oldest houses contains a mixture of both old and modern features.

name: Silver Jubilee Crystal Crown
description: A discarded movie prop that was the original monolith for "2001: A Space Odyssey."

name: Mansion House Square
description: London's amazing modernist public square that never was.

name: Giro the Dog's Grave
description: The final resting place of a German diplomat's beloved companion.

name: The Mousetrap at St. Martin's Theatre
description: The world's longest continuously running show has played at this small West End theater since 1974.

name: Kilmorey Mausoleum
description: The Egyptian-inspired last resting place of Lord Kilmorey.

name: Sotheby's Sekhmet
description: Heralded as the oldest statue in London.

name: Lincoln's Inn
description: This inn of the court provides a fascinating window into London’s legal and architectural history.

name: 'The World Turned Upside Down'
description: Soon after its unveiling, this giant inverted globe sparked controversy and was vandalized.

name: Circus Space
description: The largest clown school in Britain offering accredited degrees.

name: The Wiener Holocaust Library
description: One of the world’s leading and most extensive archives on the Holocaust, the Nazi era, and genocide, this collection includes published and unpublished works, press cuttings, photographs, and eyewitness testimony.

name: Michelin House
description: An ornate and disused early 20th-century building has been given new life as a fully operational structure, complete with offices, oyster bar, and a furniture retailer.

name: Ilford Animal Cemetery
description: 3,000 family pets and military animals are buried in this London graveyard.

name: Spitalfields Charnel House
description: Preserved behind glass, the ruins of a medieval building used to store human remains.

name: Byward Tower Hand
description: A mysterious suspended hand greets unsuspecting visitors at the Tower of London.

name: The Lioness and the Lesser Kudu
description: This striking statue brings a scene from the African savannah into central London.

name: Chi-Chi the Giant Panda
description: The beloved beast's taxidermy remains sit in the Natural History Museum's cafe.

name: Tower Hill Execution Site
description: A memorial for public executions that dates back to the 14th century.

name: Tea Building
description: A building that survived WWII, possibly on the virtue of its name alone.

name: Francis Bacon's Studio and Home
description: A plaque pays tribute to the artist's longtime London home.

name: Gravestone of Henry Croft
description: A statue of the original Pearly King is hidden in a church's crypt.

name: Wandsworth Roundabout Underpass
description: This underpass became an unlikely piece of cinematic history after its appearance in 'A Clockwork Orange.'

name: The Mount
description: The remains of a historic football stadium that once hosted 50,000 spectators.

name: The Duke of Wellington’s Mounting Stone
description: Outside of one of Britain's most renowned social clubs, a step installed specifically for the Duke.

name: Norbury Hall Park
description: This peaceful park with centuries of history hides in an overlooked section of South London.

name: Whitechapel Bell Foundry
description: This unassuming building produced some of the world's most famous bells.

name: Long John Silver Figurehead Collection
description: The world's largest collection of ship figureheads includes figures from legends and history alike, from Sir Lancelot to Abraham Lincoln.

name: Kaspar the Savoy Cat
description: This black cat was actually designed to ward off bad luck.

name: Alfred the Great Statue
description: King of the Saxons on top, Roman goddess of wisdom on the bottom.

name: Floating Coffin of Pinner
description: One man's attempt to stay one step ahead of the church.

name: Paddington Real Time Clock
description: Look up from the busy streets to discover how time is kept!

name: Giants of St. Dunstan-in-the-West
description: A pair of club-wielding guardians stand watch over London's first public clock to feature a minute hand.

name: Charing Cross Storm Tree
description: A living memorial to the trees London lost during the Great Storm of 1987.

name: Memento Mori Pendant
description: The Ranger’s House exhibits fine mementos collected by Sir Julius Werner during the course of his life, including this infamous pendant.

name: Shield of Parade
description: This magnificently macabre shield shows the power of chivalrous love in medieval times.

name: Story Deli
description: A movable feast that is an East London institution.

name: Pamela Colman Smith's Fireplace
description: Inside a London bookstore, a fireplace that once belonged to the illustrator responsible for a famous set of tarot cards.

name: Linley Sambourne House
description: Home of one of history's most famous cartoonists just as he left it.

name: Evelyn’s Mulberry
description: Legend has it this gnarled old tree was planted by Tsar Peter the Great during his visit to London.

name: Museum of Methodism
description: This religious museum resides in a church crypt next to the founder's house.

name: Wellington Monument
description: This once-controversial and slightly confusing statue of Achilles was built out of captured cannons.

name: Topolski's 'Memoir of the Century'
description: Tucked inside a South Bank bar is an epic expressionist chronicle of the 20th century.

name: Tomb of Mary Wollstonecraft
description: Visit the grave of a famous feminist author and the mother of Mary Shelley.

name: 'Peter Pan' Gravemarkers
description: Two tombstones associated with the much-beloved children's story.

name: World Trade Center Artwork
description: This sculpture, residing in a quiet corner of a London park, honors the victims of 9/11.

name: The Fourth Plinth
description: Originally meant to hold a statue of King William IV, this support sat empty for years. Today, it hosts a rotating cast of public artwork.

name: Benedict Arnold's London Residence
description: A commemorative plaque at the door pays tribute to an "American Patriot" known for his betrayal.

name: Panyer Boy
description: A mysterious carving from 17th-century London seemingly depicts a young boy sitting on a bread basket.

name: Fitzrovia Chapel
description: Designed by the architect who also had a hand in crafting the Westminster Abbey and Bristol Cathedral.

name: Town Hall Hotel
description: You will not be able to miss this building.

name: Huntsman & Sons
description: The real clothing store that inspired a fictional spy organization's front.

name: The Guinea
description: Some of the best renditions of traditional British dishes can be found on the site of a 15th-century inn.

name: Victorian Bath House
description: This 19th-century Turkish bath house survived the Blitz of London and is now an events venue.

name: Floris Perfumery
description: A storied London perfume shop that supplies scents for royalty has been operating out of this storefront for nearly 300 years.

name: Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson Plaque
description: This plaque in one of London's oldest public hospitals commemorates the first meeting of two of Britain's most famous literary heroes: Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson.

name: YMCA Indian Student Hostel
description: It’s fun to stay at the YMCA… but at this London outpost, it’s even more fun to eat the Bombay aloo.

name: Beckton Alp
description: This toxic spoil heap found a second life as a ski slope, popular film location, and wildlife haven.

name: Baltic Exchange Memorial Glass
description: These stained-glass windows are all that remains of a building that was the target of one of the biggest explosions in London after World War II.

name: Myddelton Passage Police Graffiti
description: These marks in Myddelton Passage aren't your run-of-the-mill vandalism, and they weren't made by your typical vandal.

name: Odeon Covent Garden
description: Formerly known as Saville Theatre, this mid-20th-century entertainment hub includes an ornate frieze.

name: Extreme Bike Course in Haggerston Park
description: Visit on a Sunday morning to see some killer tricks.

name: Goldsmith's Garden
description: A secluded green oasis amidst the towering skyscrapers, bears an unusual moniker that reflects one of the city's oldest guilds.

name: Square the Block
description: A jumble of concrete on the corner of one of London’s terraced buildings is actually a work of art.

name: 'The Seven Ages of Man'
description: A totem pole of heads to represent the different stages of life.

name: Barnet Museum
description: At this small museum, visitors can view artifacts from the only battlefield in London during the War of the Roses.

name: Du Cane Court
description: Legends say that this Art Deco apartment building almost became Hitler's British headquarters.

name: Meeksville Sound Plaque
description: The home studio one of Britain's first independent record producers is also the site of a murder-suicide.

name: Trellick Tower
description: This iconic high-rise residential block was one of the last to be built in the Brutalist style.

name: 'The Wisdom of Solomon' Stone Pyramid
description: This mysterious pyramid is said to be part of a complex web of occult and Freemason symbols.

name: Weaver's Field
description: This lush area was the site of extensive enemy bombing during WWII.

name: St. Bartholomew’s Gatehouse
description: One of London’s rare Tudor-era buildings is a fate-defying gatehouse for a church.

name: Arnold Circus
description: A celebrated bandstand at the center of what was once the worst slum in London.

name: Vaulty Towers
description: Run by the same people as a subterranean art space, this pub does double-duty as a Cave of Wonders.

name: Alcove From Old London Bridge
description: Before becoming a poet John Keats studied here to become a surgeon.

name: The 'Scribble'
description: A giant scrawl on the skyline of south London.

name: Endangered Species Doorway
description: An archway adorned with vulnerable flora and fauna.

name: The World Time Today
description: Tucked away in a London Tube station, this 1920s linear clock shows the time across the world all at once.

name: Bethnal Green Working Men's Club
description: Where's a guy to go after a long, hard day at work?

name: The Beaumont Hotel
description: This five-star hotel was originally a swanky parking garage.

name: George Inn
description: With a history stretching back to the 16th century, this pub has a few stories to tell.

name: The Albert Bridge
description: Chelsea's soldiers were given strict orders when crossing this historic bridge.

name: Wellington Arch
description: Once designed as the entrance to Buckingham Palace, this structure was later relocated and stands as a victory arch.

name: Wapping Hydraulic Power Station
description: This hydraulic power system in London was, at one time, seen as a rival to electricity and the last of its kind to shut down.

name: Blenheim Gardens Estate
description: A wonderfully preserved example of London's 1960s-style high-density, low-rise public housing.

name: The Gardener Statue
description: A tribute to all the green-thumbed workers who tend London’s public parks and gardens.

name: Rochelle School
description: A wellspring of creativity in an already artsy East London neighborhood.

name: Site Where the First V2 Rocket Fell on London
description: A small stone monument marks the spot on Stavely Road where the first of hundreds of missiles touched down during World War II.

name: Dog and Pot Sculpture
description: A recreation of a sign that Charles Dickens used to see on his walk home.

name: Rotherhithe Street
description: The arching stretch of London's longest street holds many reminders of the area's maritime past.

name: The Ostler's Hut
description: This is London's smallest listed building.

name: Goodman’s Fields Horses
description: Metal horses galloping through a London fountain provide a link to the city's past.

name: The Hitchcock Gallery
description: Several colorful mosaic panels depict the life and creative genius of the Master of Suspense.

name: Dorich House Museum
description: This Art Deco house is home to the world's largest collection of Dora Gordine's paintings, drawings, and sculptures.

name: Hoover Building
description: A masterpiece of Art Deco architecture hidden in plain sight in West London.

name: The Luck of Edenhall
description: A legend says that if this elaborately decorated glass breaks, the good fortune of its owner's household will also disappear.

name: National Gallery Mosaics
description: A quartet of tiled art pieces adorn the floors of this prestigious art museum.

name: Fetter Lane Moravian Burial Ground
description: Some of the earliest leaders of the Moravian congregation are buried here.

name: Site of the Watkin Tower
description: London's Victorian Tower of Babel.

name: The Dublin Castle
description: Once a 19th-century refuge for immigrants, this pub has hosted performances by Amy Winehouse and the Arctic Monkeys.

name: Southmere Lake
description: This artificial lake surrounded by Brutalist architecture served as a stand-in for futuristic Britain in Stanley Kubrick's 'A Clockwork Orange.'

name: Fleet Air Arm Memorial
description: A haunting monument dedicated to the Royal Naval Air Service and the Fleet Air Arm.

name: Torso in Metal from Rock Drill
description: This haunting sculpture was created as a statement on the mechanized warfare of the First World War.

name: Shepherdess Walk Mosaics
description: A display of everyday life in the area that was unveiled during the 2012 Summer Olympics.

name: Former Queen Elizabeth Hospital for Children
description: This hospital helped pave the way for treatment that drove down child-mortality rates in 19th and 20th centuries.

name: Umit & Son
description: A vintage media store and safe haven for cinema buffs who love 16mm films shown on vintage projectors.

name: 'Merry-Go-Round' Painting
description: A haunting painting meant to capture the calamity of World War I.

name: Eko Brewery and Taproom
description: Named for the Yoruba word for Lagos, this craft brewery draws on European beer-making and West African palm wine-fermenting techniques.

name: The Taskmaster’s House
description: A former groundskeeper’s cottage, now famous for being the main location of a hit British comedy game show.

name: 10 Hyde Park Place
description: A building claimed to be London’s smallest house has an obscure history.

name: The Meeting Place
description: This sculpture encompasses the emotion of rail travel.

name: Henry VIII’s Wine Cellar
description: Once part of a magnificent Tudor palace, this wine cellar is a time capsule underneath the Ministry of Defence.

name: Ancient Melancholy Man
description: This statue in Holland Park might be one of London’s oldest outdoor statues, but its exact origins are unknown.

name: Spencer Perceval Memorial
description: A place of remembrance for the Prime Minister that met a violent end.

name: St. Leonard’s Court Air Raid Shelter
description: This small, round building is the entrance to a bomb shelter built in anticipation of World War II.

name: Exhale: Bionic Chandelier
description: A living lighting fixture.

name: Alfred Russel Wallace Statue
description: A tribute to an explorer and naturalist often overlooked by history.

name: Victorian Penny Chute
description: An oddity of a bygone era that stills functions today.

name: Victoria Park's Old London Bridge Alcoves
description: These remnants of Old London Bridge possess unique acoustics that allow for whispered conversations to carry from one side to the other.

name: Fulham Pottery Kiln
description: Crafted by one of the first makers of stoneware in England.

name: The Bremer Car
description: Likely the first car with an internal combustion engine constructed in the United Kingdom.

name: The Euston Tap
description: The last vestiges of a magnificent Victorian-era train station can be found in this pub.

name: M. Manze
description: The oldest surviving “eel, pie, and mash” shop in London is renowned for its infamous jellied eels.

name: 'A Bullet From a Shooting Star'
description: This sculpture of a pylon is upside down and askew, as if its main purpose is to confuse passersby.

name: Grand Staircase at St. Pancras Renaissance Hotel
description: As seen in the Spice Girls ‘Wannabe’ music video.

name: Remains of Richmond Palace
description: This site was once home to Henry VIII and Elizabeth I.

name: The Catford Cat
description: For more than half a century, an enormous feline has watched over this London neighborhood.

name: Sloane Square Tube Station
description: One of London's lost rivers runs through a pipe over the tracks of this subway station.

name: The Wrong Abbey Road
description: Tourists who rely on a transit map to find the iconic Beatles location often end up in a far-off neighborhood.

name: Leg o' Mutton Reservoir and Park
description: This unusually-named reservoir found a second life as a nature preserve.

name: John Snow
description: Named for a doctor who made a breakthrough in London’s cholera epidemic, this 1800s pub was also the site of a queer “kiss-in.”

name: Sherlock Holmes Statue
description: This tribute to the fictional detective stands at the famous literary character's address.

name: Thomas Becket Plaque
description: Near the site where the English Archbishop was born, you can find an emblem in the shape of a pilgrim's badge.

name: St James’s Tiny Park
description: These former ticket office windows in London's underground now house pocket-size parks.

name: Stationers' Hall
description: This building contains a door only used by members of the royal family.

name: Tottenham Hotspur Lamppost
description: A replica of the gaslit streetlamp under which three teenagers founded a football club.

name: Gloucester Road Underground Station Disused Platform
description: The extra platform at Gloucester Road has been used for art displays for over 25 years.

name: Modern Martyrs
description: The niches over the western entrance of Westminster Abbey honor individuals from the 20th century who gave their lives for their Christian beliefs.

name: 'The Cornerstone'
description: A quirky statue in Bermondsey made by 100 public participants with goggles, chisels, and mallets.

name: Stoke Newington Town Hall
description: You can still see bits of the camouflage used to hide this Art Deco building from bombers during World War II.

name: Temple Bar
description: Over several decades, one of London’s most iconic city gates was dismantled, moved out of the city, and then moved back.

name: Of Alley and the Duke’s Streets
description: An agreement meant to honor the Duke of Buckingham led to this unusually-named passageway near Charing Cross.

name: Site of the Original Globe Theatre
description: The ground on which Shakespeare’s initial playhouse once stood.

name: London Scottish House
description: This unassuming brick building contains the last purpose-built drill hall in the United Kingdom.

name: Deptford Anchor
description: A much-loved local landmark pays tribute to the shipbuilding past of this area.

name: Edmond Halley Memorial
description: It holds a fabulously designed tribute to astronomer Edmond Halley.

name: Holborn Viaduct
description: London’s first road overpass is so old that it was originally built for horse-drawn carriages.

name: Garden Museum
description: Tony Blair's garden gnome and a Howitzer shell-turned-flower-pot are on display in this deconsecrated 13th-century church.

name: Site of the Tabard Inn
description: The location Geoffrey Chaucer and tale-telling pilgrims set off for Canterbury in April 1386.

name: Stairway to Heaven Memorial
description: An inverted staircase above the entrance to the Bethnal Green Underground Station functions as a memorial for people who lost their lives during the worst civilian disaster in the U.K. during World War II.

name: The National Covid Memorial Wall
description: This 500-meter-long memorial pays tribute to the hundreds of thousands of lives lost to COVID-19 in the UK.

name: The Molehill on The Equestrian Statue of William III
description: This statue includes the molehill that toppled a king.

name: The Southampton Arms
description: Grab a pint of British-brewed ale or cider and a hot roast pork bap at this quintessential London pub.

name: The Crypt Gallery
description: Where the dead once lay, art now plays.

name: Nimrud Lens
description: Despite the name, this ancient piece of crystal may not have been a lens at all.

name: St Antholin's Spire
description: This stone spire holds a unique place in the region's history.

name: Pimlico Priapus
description: This sanitized yet surreal depiction of a Greek fertility god looks over a square in one of central London’s residential areas.

name: ‘Take Courage’ Ghost Sign
description: This hand-painted advertisement is a literal sign of encouragement, however unintentional.

name: Royal College of Physicians Museum
description: An extraordinary collection of artifacts spanning 500 years of medical history.

name: Wimbledon Victorian Electricity Substation
description: A recently restored substation that dates from 1875.

name: Croham Hurst Woods
description: Late Mesolithic settlements and Bronze Age burials have been excavated at this ancient woodland on the top of a hill.

name: Tamesis Dock
description: Take in the views of Big Ben with a Guinness in hand at this floating pub aboard a 1930s Dutch barge.

name: St. George’s Circus
description: At the center of London’s oldest roundabout stands an obelisk that was erected in 1771, but spent several decades elsewhere.

name: Marshalsea Prison Wall
description: The remains of the debtor’s prison that shaped Charles Dickens’ life and writing.

name: Leo The Lion
description: A statue for a zoo that was never built.

name: Brick Lane Roundels
description: Art under your feet honors the history of a working-class London neighborhood.

name: Statue of James Henry Greathead
description: This statue of one of the engineers who created the London Underground hides a ventilation shaft for the tube lines that run under the street.

name: Gasholders
description: These 19th-century gas tanks were remade into an apartment complex.

name: Custard Apple, Breadfruit, and Soursop Sculptures
description: These oversized fruit sculptures honor the contributions of the Windrush generation.

name: Museum of the Royal Pharmaceutical Society
description: A Nile crocodile, a historic symbol of apothecaries, is among the fascinating collection.

name: The London Museum of Water & Steam
description: The history of West London’s water supply.

name: 186 Fleet Street
description: The (fictional) address of Sweeney Todd's bloody barbershop.

name: St. Govor’s Well
description: This ancient spring in central London was featured in a Peter Pan story.

name: Thames Barrier
description: The world’s second-largest flood defense barrier has protected London for decades.

name: Beau Brummell Statue
description: A statue honoring a preeminent 19th-century dandy and important figure of men’s fashion.

name: Kingsway Tramway Subway
description: A forgotten underground railway once whooshed trams beneath London.

name: Statue of Nell Gwynn
description: The only statue of a royal mistress in all of London.

name: Gresham College
description: England's “third-oldest college” has not graduated a single student in over 400 years.

name: Lamp Standards at Chelsea Embankment
description: Two copies of a rejected Victorian-era lamp design still stand along the Thames River.

name: Dr Salter's Daydream
description: This group of sculptures includes the first statue of a female politician in London.

name: Chingford Mount Cemetery
description: Cemetery dating back to 1884, known for being the final resting place of the notorious Kray twins.

name: Alexandra Palace Transmitter Mast
description: The first regular high-definition TV transmissions were made from this seemingly out-of-place mast located on one corner of a Victorian-era entertainment venue.

name: Oval Gasholder
description: How did a fuel storage container become one of the most famous sights in the sporting world of cricket?

name: Neuron Pod
description: A building that looks like a giant hedgehog, in the middle of East London.

name: Skanderbeg Memorial
description: The bust of a 15th-century Albanian leader in the middle of London.

name: Thomas Briggs’ Plaque
description: This blue plaque memorializes the first person to be murdered on a railway train.

name: Frank Gilbert Crichlow Plaque
description: Celebrating the man behind the Mangrove, a restaurant that became a crucial hub for Black activism in London.

name: St. George the Martyr’s Black Clockface
description: One of the four clock faces on this church is mismatched, in a nod to a historic slight.

name: ‘Three Figures’
description: A configuration of a trio of statues that depict a scene reflecting a time emblematic in the city’s rich history.

name: John Lewis Memorial at Richmond Park
description: Memorial marking the man who helped restore public rights access to the park in 1758.

name: William Roy Memorial
description: A tribute to an 18th-century British surveyor.

name: The Hardy Tree
description: This churchyard arbor is surrounded by hundreds of gravestones placed there by author Thomas Hardy.

name: Growing Underground
description: The world's first underground farm is in a World War II bomb shelter beneath the streets of London.

name: Freddie Mercury's Former Home and Studio
description: A makeshift shrine to the late rock star is still kept up on the nondescript façade outside 1 Logan Place.

name: The Buried Remains of Little Compton Street
description: The signs of a long buried road can still be found hidden beneath a London sewer grate.

name: Black Museum
description: Since 1874, this macabre collection has developed into a museum devoted to Scotland Yard's  mementos of dastardly deeds.

name: Pollock's Toy Museum
description: A toy store with a century-long history preserves the craftsmanship of playthings of the past.

name: Royal London Hospital Museum
description: This small museum in a former church crypt contains artifacts of the Elephant Man, Jack the Ripper and Florence Nightingale.

name: World's Largest Potted Plant
description: The cedar tree in Highgate Cemetery earned its title by accident.

name: Old Shoreditch Station
description: Once a train station along the Great Eastern Railway this vibrant coffee shop and bar is where members of London’s creative class mingle.

name: Kensington Roof Gardens
description: This hidden rooftop garden in downtown London offers a quiet respite from a bustling city.

name: Stompie: The Mandela Way T-34 Tank
description: An abandoned tank sits in an empty London lot as a symbolic middle finger to local city planners.

name: Suck and Chew
description: "Purveyors of traditional sweets and vintage treats."

name: PsychoBarn
description: A traditional American farmhouse plopped in the middle of a Piccadilly courtyard.

name: Davenports Magic Shop
description: The world's oldest family-run magic shop is hidden beneath a bustling London thoroughfare.

name: Clowns' Gallery and Museum
description: Three small rooms at the back of the “official” church of London's clowns.

name: Nobrow Comics
description: A comic book store that puts the marvel back in marvellous.

name: Still Life
description: Hackney's newest private museum and natural curiosity shop.

name: Kew Gardens Underground Aquarium
description: A marine aquarium with an astounding variety of fish hides in the basement beneath the famous Palm House.

name: Nomadic Community Gardens
description: This secret garden has bloomed into London's most unique hub of urban creativity.

name: Time for Tea
description: A five-story homage to tea times past.

name: George Michael Memorial
description: An unsanctioned shrine that sprung up outside the late singer's home is causing a stir in London's Highgate neighborhood.

name: 'Great Dangaroo Flood'
description: On a wall in linear London, a plaque hangs at the high water mark of one of the worst floods in fictional Kcymaerxthaereal times.

name: Genius Treasure Museum
description: This informal museum is a magical trove of anonymous art collected from car boots and flea markets.

name: Sake Dean Mahomed Plaque
description: The "shampooer of kings" who opened London's first curry cafe was a celebrity in the 19th century, but is all but forgotten now.

name: The Chalk Room
description: A high-end clothing boutique with a curious basement art installation.

name: Slate Cafe
description: If your school was decorated with trippy graffiti, served ambrosial coffee, and was frequented by mimes, it might have been similar to this groovy cafe.

name: Calvert 22
description: East London art gallery showcasing contemporary art from Europe's Eastern Bloc.

name: Hurwundeki Hair, Korean Cuisine, and Antique shop
description: Don't worry, they don't use antique scissors.
